headline: Vermont Defends Climate Superfund Law Against Industry Challenge
deck: Vermont's groundbreaking climate superfund law, designed to make fossil fuel giants cover adaptation costs, faces its critical first legal test in federal court.
body: Vermont found itself defending its pioneering 'climate superfund' law before the U.S. District Court on Monday, marking a pivotal moment in the state’s bid to hold major polluters financially accountable. This legislation aims to compel fossil fuel companies to reimburse the state for the mounting costs of climate adaptation. The legal challenge represents the first real test of Vermont’s novel strategy, drawing attention from across the nation.

At its core, Vermont's law stipulates that many of the world’s largest fossil fuel companies must contribute to the state’s climate adaptation expenses. These reimbursements are to be proportional to their fossil fuel production over the past three decades. This mechanism seeks to shift the financial burden of climate change from taxpayers to the corporations whose products have fueled global warming.

The law's passage in 2024 was deeply rooted in Vermont's immediate experience with the climate crisis, enacted between two rounds of severe statewide flooding that occurred exactly a year apart. These devastating events cast a harsh spotlight on the urgent need for robust climate resilience infrastructure. The state's legislature took direct action to address the escalating financial demands of a changing climate.

This innovative approach has drawn formidable opposition, with groups representing the fossil fuel industry leading the charge to overturn the law. They are joined by a coalition of 24 Republican state attorneys general and the Trump administration, all seeking to invalidate Vermont’s statute. This broad challenge underscores the high stakes involved in compelling corporations to pay for climate damages.

Vermont's effort emerges against a backdrop of nearly two decades of largely unsuccessful climate litigation in the U.S. Eighteen years have passed since the first 'climate tort' lawsuit was filed, yet no U.S. plaintiff has managed to collect damages for the harms of global warming. The state's superfund law represents a distinct departure from these traditional tort approaches.

“Anyone who witnessed firsthand what Vermont experienced in 2023 will understand why the legislature took this step,” Bridget Asay, one of the lawyers defending Vermont’s law, said in her closing argument on Monday.

The potential for Vermont's law to set a precedent is already evident, as New York passed its own version of a superfund law just days before the first suit challenging Vermont’s. Furthermore, at least 11 other states have considered similar legislation, signaling a growing movement among states to pursue polluters directly. This legal battle could thus reshape climate funding mechanisms nationwide.

The implications of this legal fight resonate deeply in communities across Vermont, such as Johnson, a town of 3,500 people nestled at the confluence of the Gihon and Lamoille rivers, approximately 25 miles south of Canada. Such places are on the front lines of climate change, facing recurring flood risks and the ever-increasing costs of protecting their homes and infrastructure.

The outcome of this federal court defense will ultimately determine whether states possess the legal authority to compel major polluters to contribute substantially to climate adaptation efforts. This pivotal legal test could redefine how vulnerable communities, grappling with the tangible effects of a warming planet, secure the necessary resources for their future resilience.
sources: As Vermont Defends Its Law to Make Fossil Fuel Firms Pay for Climate Adaptation, the Bill Is Already Coming Due - Inside Climate News
